subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: What FPGAs are, how they work, and where they fit in the digital design world.
keyPoints: FPGA = Field Programmable Gate Array — reconfigurable hardware
Contains: LUTs (Look-Up Tables), Flip-Flops, DSP blocks, Block RAM, I/O
LUT: implements any combinational function of N inputs — the core building block
Programming: load a bitstream to configure internal connections
vs ASIC: FPGA is slower and more expensive per unit but no NRE cost
vs CPU: FPGA runs logic in parallel — massive throughput advantage
Major vendors: Xilinx (AMD) — Vivado tool, Intel — Quartus tool
subLevels.content: An FPGA is a chip that can be programmed to implement any digital circuit. Unlike an ASIC which is permanently fabricated, an FPGA can be reprogrammed unlimited times. The core element is the LUT (Look-Up Table) — a small memory that implements any boolean function. Connect thousands of LUTs together with flip-flops and routing, and you can implement a CPU, an image processor, or any digital system. FPGAs are used in: prototyping ASICs before tapeout, high-frequency trading (low latency), telecommunications (5G base stations), aerospace/defense, and AI inference acceleration.

subLevels.title: Syntax & Patterns
subLevels.summary: Basic Verilog for FPGAs — always blocks, assign, and module structure.
keyPoints: FPGA design uses Verilog or VHDL — same RTL as ASIC flow
always_ff → maps to flip-flops on FPGA
always_comb / assign → maps to LUTs on FPGA
Vivado: Xilinx FPGA tool (xc7, UltraScale families)
Quartus: Intel FPGA tool (Cyclone, Stratix families)
Synthesis: converts RTL to FPGA primitives (LUTs, FFs)
Place & Route: maps primitives to physical FPGA resources

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Trace through a complete FPGA design flow from RTL to bitstream.
keyPoints: Step 1: Write RTL (Verilog/VHDL)
Step 2: Synthesis — RTL to FPGA netlists (LUTs, FFs)
Step 3: Constraint file (.xdc/.qsf) — assign pins and clock
Step 4: Place & Route — map to physical FPGA resources
Step 5: Timing analysis — verify setup/hold at target frequency
Step 6: Generate bitstream (.bit file)
Step 7: Program FPGA via JTAG — design runs immediately

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: Implement a parameterized LED blinker with adjustable frequency.

lab.instructions:
# Lab 0: Parameterized LED Blinker

Implement a parameterized LED blinker for a 100MHz FPGA clock:

```
module led_blink #(
  parameter CLK_HZ   = 100_000_000,
  parameter BLINK_HZ = 1
) (
  input  logic clk, rst_n,
  output logic led
);
```

Requirements:
1. Counter counts from 0 to `CLK_HZ/(2*BLINK_HZ) - 1`
2. Toggle `led` each time counter reaches its max
3. Use `$clog2` to auto-size the counter
4. Verify: at BLINK_HZ=1, CLK_HZ=100, period = 50 counts → 1Hz

Write a testbench that:
- Uses CLK_HZ=10, BLINK_HZ=1 (period=5 counts)
- Runs 20 clock cycles
- Verifies LED toggles at cycle 5 and cycle 10
hints: counter <= 0; led <= ~led;  // reset counter and toggle
lab.solutionExplanation: The LED blinker divides the clock frequency. HALF_PERIOD = CLK_HZ/(2*BLINK_HZ) counts each half-period. When counter reaches HALF_PERIOD-1, it resets and toggles LED. $clog2 automatically sizes the counter for any frequency combination.

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: Test your FPGA fundamentals.

quiz.q: What is a LUT (Look-Up Table) in an FPGA?
options: A memory block for storing data
A small truth table that implements any combinational logic function

quiz.q: FPGA vs ASIC — FPGAs are preferred when:
options: Mass production of millions of chips
Design needs to be changed after deployment or for prototyping

quiz.q: A bitstream in FPGA flow is:
options: The binary configuration file that programs the FPGA fabric
Ceiling of log base 2 of N — minimum bits to represent N

subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: XDC constraints — pin assignment, clock definition, and I/O standards.
keyPoints: XDC: Xilinx Design Constraints — Tcl-based constraint format
QSF: Quartus Settings File — Intel FPGA equivalent
Pin assignment: maps RTL port names to physical FPGA pins
Clock constraint: tells tool the frequency for timing analysis
IOSTANDARD: voltage standard for I/O pins (LVCMOS33, LVCMOS18)
SLEW: output edge rate (FAST/SLOW) affects EMI and signal integrity
DRIVE: output drive strength in mA
subLevels.content: Constraints are critical in FPGA design — without them the tool cannot verify timing or drive the correct voltage levels. The XDC (Xilinx) or QSF (Intel) file maps your RTL port names to physical chip pins. The clock constraint defines the period and waveform so the P&R tool knows what timing to meet. IOSTANDARD must match your PCB voltage rails — using LVCMOS33 on a 1.8V rail will damage the FPGA. Pin assignments come from the board schematic — always check the eval board user guide for correct pin numbers.

subLevels.title: Syntax & Patterns
subLevels.summary: XDC and QSF constraint file syntax for Vivado and Quartus.
keyPoints: create_clock -period 10 [get_ports clk] — 100MHz clock
set_property PACKAGE_PIN W5 [get_ports clk] — pin assignment
set_property IOSTANDARD LVCMOS33 [get_ports {*}] — I/O voltage
set_input_delay / set_output_delay — I/O timing constraints
set_false_path — paths to ignore in timing (async signals)
Quartus: set_location_assignment PIN_23 -to clk
Quartus: set_instance_assignment -name IO_STANDARD "3.3-V LVCMOS" -to clk
subLevels.content:
## Xilinx Vivado XDC file
## Basys3 board (xc7a35t)

## Clock — 100MHz on pin W5
create_clock -period 10.000 -name sys_clk \
             -waveform {0.000 5.000} \
             [get_ports clk]
set_property PACKAGE_PIN W5      [get_ports clk]
set_property IOSTANDARD LVCMOS33 [get_ports clk]

## Reset button (active low, center button)
set_property PACKAGE_PIN U18     [get_ports rst_n]
set_property IOSTANDARD LVCMOS33 [get_ports rst_n]

## LED outputs
set_property PACKAGE_PIN U16     [get_ports {led[0]}]
set_property PACKAGE_PIN E19     [get_ports {led[1]}]
set_property PACKAGE_PIN U19     [get_ports {led[2]}]
set_property PACKAGE_PIN V19     [get_ports {led[3]}]
set_property IOSTANDARD LVCMOS33 [get_ports {led[*]}]

## Switches inputs
set_property PACKAGE_PIN V17     [get_ports {sw[0]}]
set_property PACKAGE_PIN V16     [get_ports {sw[1]}]
set_property IOSTANDARD LVCMOS33 [get_ports {sw[*]}]

## False path for async reset
set_false_path -from [get_ports rst_n]

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Read a complete timing analysis report from Vivado.
keyPoints: WNS: Worst Negative Slack — must be ≥ 0 for timing closure
WHS: Worst Hold Slack — must be ≥ 0
TNS: Total Negative Slack — sum of all violations
Fmax: maximum frequency = 1/(clock_period - WNS)
Critical path: longest path limiting Fmax
LUT delays dominate in FPGA; routing also significant
subLevels.content:
## Vivado Timing Summary Report
## report_timing_summary

--------------------------------
Design Timing Summary
--------------------------------
WNS(ns)  TNS(ns)  WHS(ns)  WPSS(ns)
  2.314    0.000    0.187    0.000
--------------------------------
All user specified timing constraints are met.

## Critical Path Report:
Startpoint: counter_reg[23] (FF clocked by sys_clk)
Endpoint:   counter_reg[24] (FF clocked by sys_clk)
Path Group: sys_clk
Path Type:  Setup (Max at Slow Corner)

Data Path Delay: 7.686ns
  counter_reg[23]/C→Q:     0.478ns
  LUT5 (carry chain):      0.124ns  ×8
  counter_reg[24]/D:       0.000ns

Clock Path Skew: 0.000ns
Clock Uncertainty: 0.035ns

Required Time: 10.000ns
Arrival Time:   7.686ns
Slack:         +2.314ns  (MET)

## Resource Utilization:
LUTs:   28 / 20800  (0.13%)
FFs:    26 / 41600  (0.06%)

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: Write complete XDC constraints and RTL for a 4-bit switch-to-LED design.

lab.instructions:
# Lab 1: Switch-to-LED with Constraints

Design a module that shows switch values on LEDs with a registered output:

```
module sw_to_led (
  input  logic        clk,
  input  logic        rst_n,
  input  logic [3:0]  sw,
  output logic [3:0]  led
);
```

Requirements:
1. Register sw inputs on posedge clk (reduces metastability)
2. LED = registered switch value
3. Active-low reset: led = 4'b0000
4. Write the XDC constraints for Basys3:
   - Clock: pin W5, 100MHz, LVCMOS33
   - sw[0..3]: pins V17,V16,W16,W17
   - led[0..3]: pins U16,E19,U19,V19
   - All LVCMOS33
5. Testbench: verify led matches sw after 1 clock cycle
lab.solutionExplanation: Registering switch inputs prevents metastability from asynchronous button presses. The XDC file maps RTL ports to physical pins and sets I/O voltage. LVCMOS33 matches the Basys3 board's 3.3V I/O voltage. Always verify pin assignments against the board schematic.

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: Test your constraint knowledge.

options: All timing constraints are met with positive slack
Telling the tool to ignore timing on asynchronous paths

subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: FSM design on FPGAs — state encoding, one-hot vs binary, and safe FSMs.
keyPoints: FSM = Finite State Machine — fundamental FPGA design pattern
Binary encoding: N states need log2(N) flip-flops — area efficient
One-hot encoding: N states use N flip-flops — faster, more LUTs
FPGAs prefer one-hot — abundant FFs, fast next-state decode
Safe FSM: handles illegal states gracefully (returns to reset)
Mealy: outputs depend on state AND inputs — faster response
Moore: outputs depend only on state — cleaner, glitch-free
subLevels.content: FSMs are the backbone of FPGA control logic. Xilinx Vivado and Intel Quartus both have FSM extraction — they recognize state machine patterns and apply optimal encoding. On FPGAs, one-hot encoding is often preferred because FFs are abundant (FPGAs have 2 FFs per LUT), and one-hot next-state logic is simpler (one AND gate per transition). The safe state attribute tells the synthesis tool to add logic that forces the FSM back to a known state if it ever enters an undefined state — critical for reliable hardware. Always use the two-process FSM style: one for state register (sequential), one for next state and output logic (combinational).

subLevels.title: Syntax & Patterns
subLevels.summary: Two-process FSM pattern for FPGAs with one-hot encoding.
keyPoints: typedef enum logic [N-1:0] { S0=N'b1, S1=N'b10... } state_t — one-hot
Two-process style: state_reg (FF) + next_state logic (comb)
// synthesis translate_off/on — simulation-only code
/* synthesis full_case */ — tell tool all cases covered
/* synthesis parallel_case */ — all branches mutually exclusive
Vivado FSM attribute: (* fsm_encoding = "one_hot" *)
Quartus: synthesis_off pragma for debug code

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Design a UART receiver FSM step by step.
keyPoints: UART frame: 1 start bit (0) + 8 data bits + 1 stop bit (1)
States: IDLE → START → DATA[0..7] → STOP → IDLE
Baud rate: sample in middle of each bit period
Baud counter: counts CLK_HZ/BAUD_RATE clocks per bit
Data shift register: shifts in LSB first
done pulse: 1 cycle high when full byte received

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: Implement a vending machine FSM with coin detection.

lab.instructions:
# Lab 2: Vending Machine FSM

Implement a vending machine that accepts 10¢ and 25¢ coins, dispenses item at 35¢:

```
module vending_machine (
  input  logic clk, rst_n,
  input  logic coin_10,    // pulse: 10 cent inserted
  input  logic coin_25,    // pulse: 25 cent inserted
  output logic dispense,   // pulse: item dispensed
  output logic ret_10,     // pulse: return 10 cents change
  output logic [5:0] credit // current credit in cents
);
```

States: 0¢, 10¢, 20¢, 25¢, 30¢, 35¢+
- At 35¢+: dispense item, reset to 0¢
- At 35¢+ with change: assert ret_10
- Item costs 35¢ exactly

Write a testbench:
1. Insert 25¢ + 10¢ → expect dispense
2. Insert 10¢+10¢+10¢+10¢ → expect dispense + ret_10 change
hints: Change: when total > 35, assert ret_10 for 10 cents change
insert task: pulse coin for 1 cycle then deassert
lab.solutionExplanation: The vending machine FSM uses registered outputs to prevent glitches. The two-process style separates state storage (always_ff) from next-state computation (always_comb). Each coin insertion advances the state. At S35, dispense fires and the machine resets. The default case returns to S0 for safety — critical in FPGA designs.

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: Test your FSM design knowledge.

options: FPGAs have abundant FFs and one-hot next-state logic is fast with LUTs
Illegal/undefined states by returning to reset state
Current state only — no input dependency

subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: FPGA Block RAM — dual-port memory, FIFOs, and ROM inference.
keyPoints: Block RAM (BRAM): dedicated on-chip memory blocks — faster than LUTs
Simple Dual-Port: one read port, one write port — simultaneously accessible
True Dual-Port: both ports can read AND write independently
BRAM inference: Vivado/Quartus auto-infers BRAM from RTL patterns
Synchronous read: output registered — 1 cycle read latency
Asynchronous read: output combinational — avoid (bad timing)
FIFO: First-In-First-Out buffer — fundamental FPGA interface primitive
subLevels.content: Block RAMs are dedicated memory tiles on FPGAs — much more efficient than implementing RAM with LUTs. Vivado and Quartus automatically infer BRAM when they see typical memory patterns: an array with synchronous write and optionally synchronous read. True Dual-Port BRAM allows two independent ports to access the same memory simultaneously — essential for video buffers (one port writes, one reads). FIFOs are built from BRAM and are ubiquitous in FPGA designs for clock domain crossing and buffering between modules running at different rates.

subLevels.title: Syntax & Patterns
subLevels.summary: BRAM inference patterns and FIFO instantiation.
keyPoints: Simple array with sync write → infers BRAM automatically
Initial block with $readmemh/$readmemb → infers ROM
Xilinx IP Catalog: FIFO Generator — configures BRAM FIFOs
almost_full/almost_empty flags — programmable thresholds
wr_en/rd_en: write and read enable signals
full/empty: status flags — check before write/read
data_count: number of words in FIFO

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Read a FIFO timing diagram and understand full/empty flag behavior.
keyPoints: Write when NOT full: wr_en=1, full=0
Read when NOT empty: rd_en=1, empty=0
Simultaneous R+W: data count stays same if FIFO non-empty
almost_full: user-defined threshold (e.g., depth-4)
Read latency: 1 cycle for synchronous BRAM FIFO
FWFT (First-Word-Fall-Through): data available without rd_en

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: Implement a synchronous BRAM and verify read/write operation.

lab.instructions:
# Lab 3: Synchronous BRAM

Implement a 16-deep × 8-bit synchronous dual-port BRAM:

```
module bram_16x8 (
  input  logic       clk,
  input  logic       we,
  input  logic [3:0] waddr,
  input  logic [7:0] wdata,
  input  logic [3:0] raddr,
  output logic [7:0] rdata
);
```

Testbench must:
1. Write values 0xA0,0xB1,0xC2,0xD3 to addresses 0,1,2,3
2. Read back from addresses 0,1,2,3 (remember: 1-cycle read latency!)
3. Verify read data matches written data
4. Test simultaneous read and write to different addresses
5. Initialize memory with `$readmemh` pattern (comment is ok)
hints: 1-cycle latency: apply raddr at cycle N, read rdata at cycle N+1
Simultaneous R+W to different addresses is safe
lab.solutionExplanation: Synchronous BRAM has both write and read clocked. Write: when we=1, data is written on clock edge. Read: the address is registered on the clock edge and data appears one cycle later. This 1-cycle latency is the key difference from asynchronous (distributed) RAM. Vivado infers BRAM automatically from this pattern.

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: Test your BRAM and FIFO knowledge.

quiz.q: BRAM is preferred over LUT RAM because:
options: BRAM is dedicated memory — faster, denser, and doesn't consume LUTs
1 clock cycle latency — address registered, data appears next cycle
A ROM initialized with hex file contents

quiz.q: Writing to a FULL FIFO causes:
options: Write is blocked until space available

subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: DSP48 blocks — multipliers, accumulators, and FIR filters on FPGAs.
keyPoints: DSP48: dedicated multiply-accumulate block on Xilinx FPGAs
DSP48E2: 27×18 multiplier + 48-bit accumulator + pre-adder
Intel DSP: 27×27 multiplier on Stratix, 18×18 on Cyclone
Cascade: chain multiple DSP48s for wider operations
FIR filter: sum of products — maps perfectly to DSP48 cascade
Multiply inference: `a * b` in RTL → Vivado infers DSP48
Pipelining: add register stages inside DSP48 for higher Fmax
subLevels.content: DSP48 blocks are the secret weapon of FPGA performance. Every Xilinx FPGA has tens to thousands of them. A single DSP48E2 computes: P = (A ± D) × B + C in one clock cycle, where A is 30-bit, B is 18-bit, C is 48-bit, and P is 48-bit. FIR (Finite Impulse Response) filters are ideal DSP48 applications — each tap is a multiply-accumulate. A 16-tap FIR uses 16 DSP48s chained together. The key to using DSP48s efficiently is pipelining — add 3 register stages (A,B input registers + output register) to achieve >400MHz operation.

subLevels.title: Syntax & Patterns
subLevels.summary: DSP inference patterns — multiply, MAC, and FIR filter RTL.
keyPoints: a * b → infers DSP48 (if widths fit)
Always pipeline multipliers for high Fmax
MAC: P <= P + (A * B) — accumulate pattern
FIR: use generate loop for taps
// synthesis keep — prevents optimization of a signal
Vivado attribute: (* use_dsp = "yes" *) on multiplier
Saturation: clamp output to avoid overflow

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Implement a multiply-accumulate (MAC) unit and trace the pipeline.
keyPoints: MAC: P = P + A×B repeated for N cycles
Accumulator: 48-bit (prevents overflow for long sums)
Latency: 3 cycles for pipelined DSP48
Throughput: 1 result per cycle after pipeline fills
Clear signal: resets accumulator between dot products
Use signed arithmetic for signal processing

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: Implement a pipelined multiply-accumulate unit.

lab.instructions:
# Lab 4: Pipelined MAC Unit

Implement a pipelined multiply-accumulate (MAC) for dot product:

```
module mac_unit (
  input  logic        clk, rst_n,
  input  logic        clear,    // reset accumulator
  input  logic        valid_in, // input data valid
  input  logic signed [7:0]  a, b,
  output logic signed [23:0] acc_out,
  output logic               valid_out
);
```

Pipeline stages:
1. **Stage 1**: Register a, b inputs
2. **Stage 2**: Multiply a_reg × b_reg → product (16-bit)
3. **Stage 3**: Accumulate: acc += product

Testbench:
1. Compute dot product [1,2,3,4] · [5,6,7,8] = 70
2. Account for 2-cycle pipeline latency
3. Assert acc_out == 70 when valid_out=1
4. Test clear functionality
hints: Stage 2: product <= a_reg * b_reg; valid_s2 <= valid_s1;
lab.solutionExplanation: The 3-stage pipeline: input registers → multiplier → accumulator. Each stage adds one clock cycle of latency but allows the critical path to be just one stage (not all three in series). This maps perfectly to the DSP48E2 internal pipeline: A/B registers → multiply → P register. Running at 400MHz+, this computes 400 million multiply-accumulates per second.

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: Test your DSP block knowledge.

options: (A±D)×B+C in one clock — multiply-accumulate with pre-adder
Increases latency but allows higher Fmax by shortening critical path
Eliminates the need for DSP blocks

quiz.q: A FIR filter maps to DSP48s because:
options: FIR is sum of products — each tap is a multiply-accumulate

quiz.q: `a * b` in Verilog RTL when synthesized with Vivado:
options: Automatically infers DSP48 block if bit widths fit

subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: Clock domain crossing — synchronizers, FIFOs, and handshake protocols.
keyPoints: CDC: Clock Domain Crossing — signals crossing between different clocks
Metastability: FF output is undefined when setup/hold violated
Synchronizer: 2-FF chain reduces metastability probability
MTBF: Mean Time Between Failures — measures synchronizer reliability
Gray code: only 1 bit changes per transition — safe for multi-bit CDC
Async FIFO: standard solution for multi-bit CDC with flow control
False path: CDC paths must be set_false_path in constraints
subLevels.content: When a signal crosses from one clock domain to another, metastability can occur — the receiving flip-flop output is neither 0 nor 1 and can take unpredictable time to resolve. A 2-FF synchronizer gives the metastable state time to resolve before the second FF samples it, reducing failure probability to <1 error in billions of years. Single-bit signals need a 2-FF synchronizer. Multi-bit signals need either Gray-coded counters (for incrementing values) or an asynchronous FIFO (for general data). Never simply pass a multi-bit bus between clock domains without synchronization — bits can change at different times causing bus tears.

subLevels.title: Syntax & Patterns
subLevels.summary: 2-FF synchronizer, pulse synchronizer, and async FIFO pointer patterns.
keyPoints: 2-FF sync: (* ASYNC_REG = "TRUE" *) Vivado attribute
Pulse sync: stretch pulse to guarantee capture in slow domain
Gray code counter: for async FIFO read/write pointers
binary_to_gray: b ^ (b >> 1)
gray_to_binary: iterative XOR from MSB
set_false_path -from [get_cells *src_ff*] -to [get_cells *sync_ff*]
Vivado CDC analysis: report_cdc command

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Trace through an async FIFO pointer comparison for empty/full detection.
keyPoints: Write pointer in write domain, read pointer in read domain
Gray-code pointers before crossing clock domains
Synchronize gray-coded read pointer into write domain for full
Synchronize gray-coded write pointer into read domain for empty
Full: write_ptr_gray == {~read_ptr_gray_sync[MSB:MSB-1], read_ptr_gray_sync[MSB-2:0]}
Empty: read_ptr_gray == write_ptr_gray_sync

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: Implement a 2-FF synchronizer and verify metastability protection.

lab.instructions:
# Lab 5: Clock Domain Crossing Synchronizer

Implement a complete CDC package:

1. **2-FF synchronizer**:
```
module sync_2ff (
  input  logic clk_dst, rst_n,
  input  logic d_src,
  output logic q_dst
);
```

2. **Pulse synchronizer** (single clock pulse from slow to fast domain):
```
module pulse_sync (
  input  logic clk_src, clk_dst, rst_n,
  input  logic pulse_src,
  output logic pulse_dst
);
```
Hint: Toggle in src domain, 2-FF sync, edge detect in dst

3. **Testbench**:
- Use clk_src=10ns, clk_dst=7ns (different frequencies)
- Send 3 pulses in src domain
- Verify all 3 pulses received in dst domain
- Verify no double-pulses
hints: Toggle: if(pulse_src) toggle_src <= ~toggle_src — changes once per pulse
2-FF sync handles the metastability of the toggle signal
Each toggle edge in dst domain = one original pulse in src domain
lab.solutionExplanation: The toggle synchronizer converts a pulse to an edge: the toggle FF state changes once per input pulse and holds until the next pulse. The 2-FF synchronizer safely transfers this toggle across the clock boundary. Edge detection (XOR of current and delayed) converts the toggle edge back to a pulse in the destination domain. This pattern reliably transfers any number of pulses regardless of the clock frequency ratio.

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: Test your CDC knowledge.

options: A flip-flop's setup/hold time is violated — output is undefined

quiz.q: Gray code is used in async FIFO pointers because:
options: Only 1 bit changes per count — safe to sample across clock domains
Places synchronizer FFs in the same slice for best timing

quiz.q: For a multi-bit bus CDC, the correct approach is:
options: Use 2-FF synchronizer on each bit separately
Use Gray coding or async FIFO

subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: AXI4 protocol — the standard FPGA interconnect for Xilinx/Intel IP.
keyPoints: AXI4: ARM AMBA Advanced eXtensible Interface — industry standard
AXI4-Full: high-performance burst memory-mapped interface
AXI4-Lite: simple, single-transfer register interface
AXI4-Stream: unidirectional streaming without address
5 channels: AW, W, B (write), AR, R (read)
VALID/READY handshake: both must be high for transfer
Xilinx Vivado: IP Integrator uses AXI for all IP connections
subLevels.content: AXI is the backbone of modern FPGA designs. Xilinx Zynq (ARM+FPGA) uses AXI to connect the processor to FPGA logic. Every Xilinx IP block has AXI interfaces. AXI4-Lite is for control registers — simple read/write of single 32-bit values. AXI4-Full supports burst transactions (up to 256 beats) for high-bandwidth memory access. AXI4-Stream is for data streaming — video, audio, network packets — where there's no address, just continuous data flow with TVALID/TREADY/TDATA/TLAST. Understanding AXI is mandatory for any serious FPGA work.

subLevels.title: Syntax & Patterns
subLevels.summary: AXI4-Lite slave and AXI4-Stream passthrough implementation patterns.
keyPoints: AXI4-Lite slave: state machine for write and read channels
Write: AW+W channels → B response
Read: AR channel → R channel
BVALID/BREADY: write response handshake
RVALID/RREADY: read data handshake
AXI-Stream: TVALID, TREADY, TDATA, TKEEP, TLAST
TLAST: marks end of packet/frame in stream

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Trace an AXI4-Stream data path through an FPGA design.
keyPoints: Source: camera/ADC drives TVALID+TDATA+TLAST
Processing: AXI-Stream passthrough with FIFO buffering
Sink: HDMI output or DDR memory accepts stream
Back-pressure: sink asserts TREADY=0 to pause source
TLAST: marks end of video line or network packet
TKEEP: byte enables for partial last beat

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: Implement an AXI4-Lite slave with 4 readable/writable registers.

lab.instructions:
# Lab 6: AXI4-Lite Slave Registers

Implement an AXI4-Lite slave with 4 32-bit registers:
- Reg 0 (0x00): Control register (R/W)
- Reg 1 (0x04): Status register (R only, driven by HW)
- Reg 2 (0x08): Data register (R/W)
- Reg 3 (0x0C): ID register (R only, hardcoded 0xDEAD_C0DE)

Simplified interface (no burst, single transfer):
```
module axi_lite_regs (
  input  logic        clk, rst_n,
  input  logic [3:0]  awaddr, araddr,
  input  logic        awvalid, wvalid, arvalid, bready, rready,
  input  logic [31:0] wdata,
  input  logic [3:0]  hw_status,  // hardware status input
  output logic        awready, wready, bvalid, arready, rvalid,
  output logic [31:0] rdata,
  output logic [1:0]  bresp, rresp,
  output logic [31:0] ctrl_reg, data_reg  // to rest of design
);
```

Testbench: write 0xABCD to ctrl_reg, read it back; read ID reg = 0xDEADC0DE
hints: awaddr[3:2] gives word address (0,1,2,3 for 4 registers)
Write: accept when awvalid && wvalid both high
Read: register address on arvalid, respond with rvalid
lab.solutionExplanation: AXI4-Lite slave separates write (AW+W+B channels) and read (AR+R channels). Write accepts address and data when both AWVALID and WVALID are high, then responds with BVALID. Read accepts address when ARVALID, then drives RDATA with RVALID. Read-only registers (status, ID) ignore writes. This is the standard pattern for FPGA control register banks.

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: Test your AXI knowledge.

options: Simple register read/write — control and status registers
Both VALID and READY are high simultaneously
End of packet/frame — last beat of a transaction

subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: HLS — High-Level Synthesis, writing FPGA hardware in C/C++.
keyPoints: HLS: compile C/C++ code to RTL automatically
Xilinx Vitis HLS (formerly Vivado HLS): industry-leading tool
Intel HLS Compiler: alternative for Intel FPGAs
Directives (#pragma HLS): guide the HLS tool for optimization
Pipeline: II=1 means new data accepted every clock cycle
Unroll: expand loops to increase parallelism
Dataflow: pipeline function calls at coarse grain level
subLevels.content: HLS allows software engineers to describe hardware behavior in C/C++ and let the tool generate RTL. The key insight: HLS doesn't make your C code run faster — it makes it run in parallel hardware. The HLS tool analyzes data dependencies and creates pipelines, parallel units, and memories. Directives (#pragma HLS PIPELINE, #pragma HLS UNROLL) guide the tool. A simple C loop with II=1 (Initiation Interval=1) means the hardware accepts a new input every clock cycle — achieving hardware-level throughput without writing FSMs and datapaths manually. Used heavily for: machine learning inference, image processing, wireless algorithms.

subLevels.title: Syntax & Patterns
subLevels.summary: HLS C++ patterns — pragmas, interfaces, and data types.
keyPoints: #pragma HLS PIPELINE II=1 — pipeline with II=1 (1 new input/cycle)
#pragma HLS UNROLL factor=4 — unroll loop 4x
#pragma HLS ARRAY_PARTITION complete dim=1 — split array into regs
ap_int<N>: arbitrary precision integer (N-bit)
ap_fixed<W,I>: fixed-point (W total bits, I integer bits)
hls::stream<T>: FIFO stream in HLS — maps to AXI-Stream
DATAFLOW pragma: pipeline function calls

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Understand HLS pipeline scheduling and resource estimation.
keyPoints: HLS schedule: assigns operations to clock cycles
II=1: new data every cycle (fully pipelined)
Latency: cycles from input to output (typically 5-15)
Resource estimate: DSPs, BRAMs, LUTs after HLS
ARRAY_PARTITION: splits array to allow parallel access
Bottleneck: RAW dependency (read-after-write) limits II

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: Write an HLS vector dot product with pipeline and unroll pragmas.

lab.instructions:
# Lab 7: HLS-Style Dot Product

Since we're simulating HLS concepts in SystemVerilog, implement a fully pipelined dot product that demonstrates HLS principles:

```
module dot_product #(parameter N=4) (
  input  logic        clk, rst_n,
  input  logic        valid_in,
  input  logic signed [7:0] a [N],
  input  logic signed [7:0] b [N],
  output logic signed [23:0] result,
  output logic               valid_out
);
```

Implement with full parallelism (like HLS UNROLL):
1. **Cycle 1**: All N multiplications in parallel
2. **Cycle 2**: Adder tree (parallel adds)
3. **Cycle 3**: Final sum → result

For N=4: [1,2,3,4]·[5,6,7,8] = 5+12+21+32 = 70

Testbench: compute 3 dot products back-to-back, verify all = 70
hints: Stage 1: all multiplications in one always_ff (parallel like HLS UNROLL)
3 pipeline stages = 3 cycle latency = results start 3 cycles after input
lab.solutionExplanation: This parallel dot product implements HLS UNROLL semantics in RTL. Stage 1: 4 DSP multipliers operate simultaneously. Stage 2: 2 adders reduce 4 products to 2 sums. Stage 3: final addition. Total: 3 cycles latency, 1 new result per cycle throughput (after pipeline fills). HLS PIPELINE II=1 + UNROLL generates exactly this hardware automatically from C code.

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: Test your HLS knowledge.

options: New data accepted every 1 clock cycle — fully pipelined
Replicates loop body in parallel hardware — increases throughput
Arbitrary precision N-bit integer — maps to exact-width hardware

quiz.q: HLS ARRAY_PARTITION complete is needed because:
options: Arrays are stored as BRAM with 1 read/write port — partition splits to registers for parallel access

subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: FPGA timing closure — achieving target Fmax with retiming and pipelining.
keyPoints: Timing closure: making all paths meet setup and hold requirements
Fmax: maximum operating frequency — limited by critical path
Retiming: tool moves registers across combinational logic for balance
Pipeline: manually add register stages to break long paths
register_retiming: Vivado synthesis option to enable retiming
Fanout: high fanout nets have routing delay — add buffers
Placement constraints: lock critical path logic to nearby cells
subLevels.content: Timing closure in FPGA design is the art of making every path meet its timing requirement. The critical path — the longest combinational path between flip-flops — limits Fmax. Strategies: pipelining (add registers to break long paths), retiming (tool moves registers automatically), and physical optimization (place critical cells close together). High fanout is a common timing killer — a single register driving 1000 LUTs has high routing delay. Insert buffers or use set_max_fanout constraint. Vivado's physical optimization (phys_opt_design) can fix many timing issues automatically by duplicating cells or adjusting placement.

subLevels.title: Syntax & Patterns
subLevels.summary: Vivado timing analysis commands and optimization techniques.
keyPoints: report_timing_summary: shows WNS, TNS, WHS
report_timing -max_paths 10 -path_type full: detailed paths
phys_opt_design: physical optimization after P&R
set_property KEEP_HIERARCHY false [get_cells]: enables cross-hierarchy optimization
pipeline_style: attribute to control pipeline behavior
set_multicycle_path: for intentionally slow paths
Vivado: synth_design -retiming — enable retiming
subLevels.content:
# Vivado Timing Optimization Flow

# Step 1: Synthesis with retiming
synth_design -top my_design \
             -part xc7a35tcpg236-1 \
             -retiming

# Step 2: Implementation
opt_design
place_design
phys_opt_design  ;# physical optimization
route_design

# Step 3: Check timing
report_timing_summary -file timing_summary.rpt

# Step 4: If violations exist, view critical path
report_timing -max_paths 5 \
              -path_type full \
              -delay_type max \
              -file critical_path.rpt

# Step 5: Post-route physical optimization
# (fixes remaining violations by cell duplication)
phys_opt_design -directive AggressiveExplore

# Step 6: Common fixes in RTL
# - Add pipeline register to long path
# - Use (* keep = "true" *) to prevent optimization issues
# - set_max_fanout 8 for control signals
# - PBLOCK constraint to co-locate critical cells

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Fix a timing violation by adding a pipeline register.
keyPoints: Original: 32-bit adder + 8:1 mux = 15ns critical path at 100MHz
Violation: WNS = -5ns (need 10ns, have 15ns combinational)
Fix: add pipeline register between adder and mux
Result: adder = 8ns, mux = 6ns — both meet 10ns
Trade-off: 1 extra cycle latency
Pipeline throughput: unchanged — still 1 result/cycle

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: Add pipeline registers to fix a multi-stage timing violation.

lab.instructions:
# Lab 8: Pipeline Register Insertion

The following design has a 3-stage combinational path that's too slow:

```
// Critical path: multiply → shift → accumulate = TOO LONG
module slow_path (
  input  logic        clk,
  input  logic [7:0]  a, b,
  input  logic [2:0]  shift_amt,
  output logic [23:0] result
);
  logic [15:0] product;
  logic [23:0] shifted;
  assign product = a * b;           // ~8ns
  assign shifted = product << shift_amt; // ~4ns  
  // Total combinational: 12ns — too slow for 10ns clock!
  always_ff @(posedge clk)
    result <= shifted + result;   // + 3ns = 15ns VIOLATION
endmodule
```

Fix by adding pipeline registers to split into 2 stages:
- **Stage 1**: multiply only (8ns)
- **Stage 2**: shift + accumulate (7ns)

Testbench: verify pipelined version gives same final result as combinational (accounting for latency). Compute: a=4, b=5, shift=2 → product=20, shifted=80, accumulated over 3 cycles.
hints: Stage 1: product_reg <= a * b; (register the multiply output)
Always register shift_amt alongside product (same cycle)
lab.solutionExplanation: The pipeline register breaks the 15ns path into two 7-8ns stages. Stage 1 computes and registers the multiply result. Stage 2 shifts and accumulates. Each stage fits within the 10ns clock period. The shift_amt register is critical — it must be delayed by the same number of cycles as the data to stay aligned. Result: same computation, 1 cycle extra latency, meets timing.

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: Test your timing closure knowledge.

quiz.q: Adding a pipeline register to fix timing:
options: Increases latency by 1 cycle but maintains throughput

quiz.q: High fanout signals have timing problems because:
options: Routing delay increases with more loads — signal reaches each destination at different times
Performs physical optimization post-P&R — duplicates cells, adjusts placement to fix timing
Automatically moves registers across combinational logic to balance path delays

subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: Zynq SoC — combining ARM processor with FPGA fabric on one chip.
keyPoints: Zynq: ARM Cortex-A9 (PS) + Artix/Kintex FPGA (PL) on one die
PS: Processing System — runs Linux, bare-metal, FreeRTOS
PL: Programmable Logic — your custom FPGA design
AXI: PS and PL communicate via AXI interconnect
HP ports: High Performance AXI ports — PS DDR access from PL
GP ports: General Purpose AXI — PS controls PL registers
Petalinux: Xilinx embedded Linux for Zynq
subLevels.content: Zynq combines the best of both worlds: a hard ARM processor for software algorithms and FPGA fabric for hardware acceleration. The PS runs operating systems (Linux, FreeRTOS) while the PL implements custom hardware accelerators. They communicate via AXI: the PS can read/write PL registers (AXI-GP), and PL hardware can access PS DDR memory at high bandwidth (AXI-HP at >1GB/s). Common use cases: video processing (PS controls, PL does pixel manipulation), motor control (PS runs RTOS, PL does PWM/encoder), machine learning (PS coordinates, PL runs inference). Vivado IP Integrator (block design) makes Zynq designs visual and manageable.

subLevels.title: Syntax & Patterns
subLevels.summary: Zynq PS-PL interface — AXI GP and HP port patterns.
keyPoints: Block Design: Vivado IP Integrator — visual AXI connections
ZYNQ7 Processing System IP: configures PS clocks, DDR, MIO
AXI Interconnect IP: routes multiple AXI masters/slaves
PS to PL: AXI-GP master (PS) → AXI-Lite slave (PL registers)
PL to DDR: AXI-HP slave (PS) ← AXI-Full master (PL DMA)
devmem: Linux command to read/write AXI-mapped registers
mmap: C code to map PL registers into Linux user space

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Read a complete Zynq block design and understand PS-PL data flow.
keyPoints: ZYNQ7 PS IP: configure DDR, clocks (100MHz PL clock), UART
AXI Interconnect: M00_AXI → LED controller, S00_AXI from PS
Address map: PS maps PL peripherals at 0x43C0_0000
DMA: PS DMA IP transfers data from DDR to PL stream
ILA: Integrated Logic Analyzer — debug PL signals from Vivado
subLevels.content:
// Zynq Block Design — IP connections (Vivado TCL)

# Create PS IP
create_bd_cell -type ip \
  -vlnv xilinx.com:ip:processing_system7 ZYNQ7_Processing_System

# Configure PS: enable M_AXI_GP0, set PL clock to 100MHz
set_property -dict [
  list CONFIG.PCW_FPGA0_PERIPHERAL_FREQMHZ {100} \
       CONFIG.PCW_USE_M_AXI_GP0 {1} \
       CONFIG.PCW_USE_S_AXI_HP0 {1}
] [get_bd_cells ZYNQ7_Processing_System]

# Add AXI Interconnect
create_bd_cell -type ip \
  -vlnv xilinx.com:ip:axi_interconnect axi_interconnect_0
set_property CONFIG.NUM_MI {2} [get_bd_cells axi_interconnect_0]

# Connect PS GP0 → Interconnect → LED controller
connect_bd_intf_net \
  [get_bd_intf_pins ZYNQ7_Processing_System/M_AXI_GP0] \
  [get_bd_intf_pins axi_interconnect_0/S00_AXI]

# Address assignment: LED controller at 0x43C00000
assign_bd_address -offset 0x43C00000 -range 4K \
  [get_bd_addr_segs led_ctrl/s_axi/reg0]

# From Linux:
# echo 0x0F > /sys/class/led_ctrl/leds  # turn on 4 LEDs
# devmem 0x43C00000 w 0x0000000F

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: Implement a Zynq-compatible AXI-Lite peripheral with interrupt.

lab.instructions:
# Lab 9: Zynq AXI-Lite Peripheral with Interrupt

Implement a timer peripheral for Zynq PS connection:

```
module zynq_timer (
  input  logic        clk, rst_n,
  // AXI-Lite (simplified)
  input  logic [3:0]  awaddr, araddr,
  input  logic        awvalid, wvalid, arvalid, bready, rready,
  input  logic [31:0] wdata,
  output logic        awready, wready, bvalid, arready, rvalid,
  output logic [31:0] rdata,
  output logic [1:0]  bresp, rresp,
  // Interrupt to PS
  output logic        irq
);
```

Register map:
- 0x0: CTRL — bit0=enable, bit1=auto_reload
- 0x4: LOAD — timer reload value
- 0x8: COUNT — current count (read only)
- 0xC: STATUS — bit0=expired (write 1 to clear)

Behavior:
- When enabled: count down from LOAD to 0
- At 0: assert irq for 1 cycle, set STATUS[0]=1
- If auto_reload: reload LOAD value and continue
- Testbench: set LOAD=10, enable, wait for irq, verify STATUS
hints: Timer: if(count_reg==0) fire irq, else count_reg <= count_reg-1
Preload: when disabled, count_reg = load_reg (ready to start)
irq <= 0 as default, irq <= 1 only on expiry
lab.solutionExplanation: The Zynq timer peripheral combines AXI-Lite register access with a countdown timer and interrupt. W1C (Write-1-to-Clear) status is the standard pattern for interrupt status registers. The PS writes to LOAD and CTRL via AXI, the hardware timer counts down and fires IRQ, and the PS interrupt handler reads STATUS and clears it. This is how all hardware peripherals (UART, SPI, I2C) work in embedded systems.

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: Test your Zynq knowledge.

options: Processing System — the ARM processor subsystem

quiz.q: AXI-HP ports on Zynq are used for:

Interrupt status flags — write 1 to clear the flag

quiz.q: Vivado IP Integrator Block Design is used to:

subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: Industry FPGA projects — HFT, 5G, video processing, and AI accelerators.
keyPoints: HFT: High-Frequency Trading — FPGA processes orders in <1μs (faster than CPU)
5G baseband: FPGA implements LDPC/Polar decoders at wire speed
Video: FPGA processes 4K@60fps — 8GB/s pixel throughput
AI inference: FPGA runs CNN/transformer layers at low latency
Partial reconfiguration: change part of FPGA without full restart
OpenCL/SYCL: high-level FPGA programming for data center FPGAs
SmartNIC: FPGA-based network card with programmable packet processing
subLevels.content: Industry FPGA engineers work on performance-critical systems where FPGAs have unique advantages. In HFT, network packets are processed by FPGA in 100-500ns — far below the microseconds a CPU would take. In 5G, the tight real-time requirements of the physical layer (1ms latency) are only achievable with FPGA parallelism. Video processing exploits FPGA's streaming capability — pixels flow through a pipeline at 1 pixel per clock cycle. AI inference on FPGA offers the latency of GPU with the flexibility of reprogramming. Understanding the application domain and translating requirements to FPGA architecture is the core skill.

subLevels.title: Syntax & Patterns
subLevels.summary: Advanced FPGA patterns — partial reconfiguration, ILA debug, and timing exceptions.
keyPoints: ILA (Integrated Logic Analyzer): sample internal signals on FPGA
VIO (Virtual I/O): drive/read signals from Vivado GUI
Partial reconfig: pblock defines reconfigurable region
MMCM/PLL: generate precise clocks from reference (jitter <50ps)
IBUFDS: differential input buffer for LVDS/PCIe
OBUFDS: differential output buffer
IDELAYE2: programmable input delay for DDR training

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Design a video pixel processing pipeline for 1080p60.
keyPoints: 1080p60: 1920×1080 pixels at 60fps = 124.4 million pixels/sec
Pixel clock: 148.5MHz (standard HDMI pixel clock)
Pipeline: input → RGB conversion → filter → output
Back-pressure: TREADY handles flow control
Line buffer: store one image line for 2D filtering
Blanking: H/V blanking periods in video timing

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: Implement a complete video pixel pipeline with RGB-to-grayscale conversion.

lab.instructions:
# Lab 10: Video Pixel Pipeline

Implement a 3-stage video processing pipeline:

**Stage 1**: RGB → Grayscale (2 cycles)
- Gray = (77×R + 150×G + 29×B) >> 8

**Stage 2**: Threshold (1 cycle)
- if gray > threshold → pixel=white (255)
- else → pixel=black (0)

**Stage 3**: Output with metadata
- Output: pixel, valid, hsync, vsync
- Propagate hsync/vsync through pipeline

```
module video_pipeline (
  input  logic        clk, rst_n,
  input  logic        valid_in, hsync_in, vsync_in,
  input  logic [7:0]  r, g, b,
  input  logic [7:0]  threshold,
  output logic        valid_out, hsync_out, vsync_out,
  output logic [7:0]  pixel_out
);
```

Testbench: send 5 pixels, verify gray conversion and threshold.
hints: Propagate hsync/vsync through EVERY pipeline stage
lab.solutionExplanation: Video pipelines process pixels at the pixel clock rate — no buffering allowed between stages. Each stage registers its outputs (pipelined), achieving 1 pixel/cycle throughput. Sync signals (hsync, vsync) must be delayed through the same number of pipeline stages as data to stay aligned with their pixels. This 3-stage pipeline can process 148MHz pixels — sufficient for 1080p60.

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: Test your industry FPGA knowledge.

options: FPGA processes orders in <1μs — far faster than software on CPU

quiz.q: ILA (Integrated Logic Analyzer) is used for:
options: Capturing and viewing internal FPGA signals on real hardware

quiz.q: In video pipelines, hsync/vsync must be:
options: Delayed through the same number of pipeline stages as pixel data

quiz.q: MMCM on Xilinx FPGAs is used to:
options: Generate precise clock frequencies from a reference clock

subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: What FPGA interviews at top companies actually test.
keyPoints: RTL coding: write FSM, FIFO, or protocol in 45 minutes
Timing: explain critical path, WNS, and how to fix violations
BRAM: when to use Block RAM vs distributed RAM vs registers
CDC: explain 2-FF synchronizer and when you need Gray code
AXI: explain VALID/READY handshake and transaction flow
Companies: Xilinx/AMD, Intel, Qualcomm, NVIDIA, Broadcom, HFT firms
Common question: implement a FIFO or UART in RTL
subLevels.content: FPGA interviews test RTL coding ability above all else. You must write clean, synthesizable Verilog/SystemVerilog quickly and correctly. The most common interview questions: (1) implement a synchronous FIFO with full/empty flags, (2) implement a UART transmitter, (3) explain CDC and draw a 2-FF synchronizer, (4) given a timing report, identify the critical path and propose a fix, (5) compare FPGA to ASIC. Know your tools: Vivado for Xilinx, Quartus for Intel. Know what maps to what resource: logic → LUT, state → FF, memory → BRAM, math → DSP48.

subLevels.title: Common Interview Patterns
subLevels.summary: Top 10 FPGA coding patterns asked in every interview.
keyPoints: 1. Synchronous FIFO with full/empty — the classic interview question
2. UART TX/RX — serial protocol implementation
3. SPI master — 4-wire serial interface
4. PWM generator — duty cycle control
5. Debouncer — button/switch metastability filter
6. Edge detector — rising/falling edge detection
7. Frequency counter — measure unknown clock
8. Shift register — LFSR, delay line
9. Priority encoder — first-1 detector
10. Clock divider — integer and fractional

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Solve 5 classic FPGA interview questions.
keyPoints: Q: FPGA vs ASIC — when to choose each?
Q: What maps to DSP48 blocks?
Q: How do you cross clock domains safely?
Q: Given timing violation WNS=-3ns, what do you do?
Q: Implement a 4-bit Gray code counter

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: Classic FPGA interview challenge — synchronous FIFO with full/empty flags.

lab.instructions:
# Lab 11: Classic Interview — Synchronous FIFO

Implement a parameterized synchronous FIFO:

```
module sync_fifo #(
  parameter DATA_W = 8,
  parameter DEPTH  = 16
) (
  input  logic             clk, rst_n,
  input  logic             wr_en, rd_en,
  input  logic [DATA_W-1:0] wr_data,
  output logic [DATA_W-1:0] rd_data,
  output logic             full, empty,
  output logic [$clog2(DEPTH):0] count
);
```

**Requirements:**
1. BRAM storage (sync read — 1 cycle latency)
2. Full: count == DEPTH
3. Empty: count == 0
4. Count tracks number of valid words
5. No write when full, no read when empty

**Testbench:**
1. Fill FIFO completely — verify full=1
2. Read all — verify empty=1
3. Write and read simultaneously — count stays constant
4. Verify data order (FIFO ordering)
hints: wr_ptr and rd_ptr are $clog2(DEPTH)-bit pointers that wrap naturally
count: +1 on write only, -1 on read only, unchanged on both
lab.solutionExplanation: The synchronous FIFO is the most common FPGA interview question. Key elements: BRAM storage (mem array), write pointer (wr_ptr), read pointer (rd_ptr), word count, full/empty flags. The count register tracks occupancy — increment on write, decrement on read, unchanged on simultaneous R+W. Full when count==DEPTH, empty when count==0. $clog2 auto-sizes pointers for any depth.

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: FPGA interview-style questions.

quiz.q: Most common FPGA interview coding question:
options: Implement a synchronous FIFO with full/empty flags
A LUT comparator — duty-cycle proportional high output
Current signal XOR delayed signal — current^delayed

quiz.q: Gray code counter advantage over binary:
options: Only 1 bit changes per count — safe for CDC

subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: Complete FPGA system — the final boss challenge.
keyPoints: Full system: AXI-Lite control + AXI-Stream data + BRAM storage + DSP processing
Architecture: PS controls via AXI-Lite → PL accelerator processes data → DMA to DDR
Verification: UVM-like testbench with scoreboard for FPGA designs
Performance: calculate throughput, latency, resource utilization
Power: dynamic (switching) + static (leakage) — Vivado power analysis
Constraints: multi-clock, CDC paths, I/O timing all need constraints
Deployment: bitstream + device tree + Linux driver for full system
subLevels.content: The FPGA architect designs the complete system from requirements to deployment. Given a spec ('process 100 million samples/second, latency <10μs, fit in xc7a100t'), the architect determines: how many pipeline stages, what clock frequency, which blocks use DSP48/BRAM/LUT, how PS and PL communicate, what constraints are needed. The final design is a working FPGA system that can be deployed on real hardware — not just simulated. This requires mastery of all previous levels: RTL, constraints, timing closure, memory interfaces, DSP, AXI, and debugging.

subLevels.title: Architecture Overview
subLevels.summary: Complete FPGA system architecture connecting all components.
keyPoints: Top level: connects PS interface + accelerator + memory
AXI-Lite: configuration registers from PS
AXI-Stream: data path — source → accelerator → sink
BRAM: coefficient storage and ping-pong buffers
DSP: FIR filter / correlation / FFT butterfly
ILA: embedded debug for all major interfaces
Clock: MMCM generates processing clock from PS clock

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Read a complete FPGA system performance and resource analysis.
keyPoints: Fmax: 200MHz achieved — meets 200MHz target
Throughput: 200M samples/sec (1 sample/cycle at 200MHz)
Latency: 8 taps + 2 pipeline = 10 cycles = 50ns at 200MHz
DSP48: 8 used (one per FIR tap) of 90 available
BRAM: 1 used for coefficients of 50 available
LUT: 450 used (2.2% of xc7a100t)
subLevels.content:
# Complete System Analysis — 8-tap FIR Accelerator
# Target FPGA: xc7a100t (Artix-7 100T)

## Timing Summary
Clock frequency:  200 MHz (5ns period)
WNS:             +0.847 ns  → Fmax = 214 MHz  PASS
WHS:             +0.312 ns  PASS

## Throughput Analysis
Samples/cycle:     1 (fully pipelined, II=1)
Clock frequency:   200 MHz
Throughput:        200 million samples/second
At 16-bit: 200M × 16-bit = 3.2 Gbps

## Latency Analysis
FIR taps: 8 (shift register depth)
Pipeline stages: 2 (multiply + accumulate)
Total latency: 8+2 = 10 clock cycles = 50ns ✓ (target: <100ns)

## Resource Utilization
LUTs:    450 / 63400  (0.71%)  ← control logic
FFs:     512 / 126800 (0.40%)  ← pipeline registers
DSP48:     8 / 240    (3.33%)  ← FIR multipliers
BRAM:      1 / 135    (0.74%)  ← coefficient storage

## Power Estimate (Vivado)
Dynamic: 0.842W  (switching activity at 200MHz)
Static:  0.100W  (quiescent leakage)
Total:   0.942W  ← well within 5W budget

## AXI Performance
AXI-Stream throughput: 200M × 8-bit = 1.6 Gbps
AXI-Lite latency: 2 cycles read, 1 cycle write

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: FINAL BOSS — Complete FPGA system: FIR accelerator + AXI control + BRAM coefficients.

lab.instructions:
# Lab 12: FINAL BOSS — Complete FPGA Accelerator System

Build a complete 8-tap FIR accelerator with AXI control:

**Components:**
1. **AXI-Lite slave** — 4 registers: CTRL, STATUS, COEFF_ADDR, COEFF_DATA
2. **Coefficient BRAM** — 8 × 8-bit coefficients, writable via AXI
3. **8-tap FIR filter** — pipelined, 1 sample/cycle throughput
4. **AXI-Stream interface** — input/output data streams

**Top module:**
```
module fir_accelerator (
  input  logic        clk, rst_n,
  // AXI-Lite control
  input  logic [3:0]  s_awaddr, s_araddr,
  input  logic        s_awvalid, s_wvalid, s_arvalid,
  input  logic        s_bready, s_rready,
  input  logic [31:0] s_wdata,
  output logic        s_awready, s_wready, s_bvalid,
  output logic        s_arready, s_rvalid,
  output logic [31:0] s_rdata,
  output logic [1:0]  s_bresp, s_rresp,
  // AXI-Stream input
  input  logic        s_tvalid,
  input  logic signed [7:0] s_tdata,
  output logic        s_tready,
  // AXI-Stream output
  output logic        m_tvalid,
  output logic signed [15:0] m_tdata
);
```

**Testbench:**
1. Write coefficients [1,2,3,4,4,3,2,1] via AXI-Lite
2. Enable accelerator (CTRL[0]=1)
3. Stream 12 samples [1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8,0,0,0,0]
4. Verify FIR output for samples 7-11 (pipeline settled)
hints: FIR: delay line shifts samples, multiply all taps in parallel
Pipeline: 2 stages = 2 cycle latency after first valid input
Enable via AXI write to addr 0x0 with data=1
lab.solutionExplanation: The complete FIR accelerator integrates AXI-Lite control, BRAM coefficient storage, and a pipelined FIR engine into one system. AXI-Lite enables PS to configure and enable the accelerator. The FIR core uses a delay line (shift register → BRAM) and parallel multipliers (→ DSP48). AXI-Stream carries data at line rate. This architecture is used in real FPGA signal processing designs at Qualcomm, Intel, and Xilinx/AMD.
